theme: The Real Cost of NOT Doing an Incentive Trip

linkedin.text:
Most budget conversations about incentive travel start with the wrong number.

Leaders ask: 'What does the trip cost?'
The better question is: 'What does losing this person cost?'

Replacing one experienced employee runs 50–200 % of their annual salary — search fees, ramp time, team disruption, institutional knowledge that walks out with them. That's before you account for the ripple effect on the team that stayed and watched.

Over 16 years and 1,500+ events, I've sat across the table from HR directors and CFOs having this exact conversation. The companies that treat incentive travel as a retention investment — not a reward budget — consistently report lower voluntary attrition the following year.

The math isn't complicated. What's complicated is getting procurement to see people costs the same way they see software licenses.

If you're building the internal case for next year's incentive programme, drop a comment or DM me — happy to share how peers in your sector are framing it. No pitch, just the framework.

instagram.text:
The most expensive event is the one you didn't run. 📊

When a high performer walks out the door, the real bill — recruiting, onboarding, lost momentum — often lands between 50 and 200 % of their salary.

The incentive trip your team is still debating? It's not a cost line. It's a retention strategy with a receipt.

16 years. 1,500+ events. 130+ destinations. 25,000+ participants.
We help companies run the numbers before they run the risk.

Save this post if you're building next year's people-investment case. 💼

→ DM us 'ROI' and we'll walk you through how top HR teams frame the business case.

facebook.text:
Here's a number that stops most HR leaders mid-scroll:

Replacing a single mid-level employee costs roughly 50–200 % of their annual salary — recruitment, onboarding, lost productivity, knowledge drain.

A well-designed incentive trip for a team of 50? A fraction of that.

We've delivered 1,500+ events to 25,000+ participants across 130+ destinations. The clients who keep coming back aren't doing it because it 'feels nice.' They're doing it because they've done the math.

What does turnover actually cost your company right now? Drop a number in the comments — I'm curious what people are tracking. 👇
